skill.name: professional-senior-chrome-extension-architect-developer
skill.description: Guides agents through secure Chrome Manifest V3 extension architecture, TypeScript implementation, AI integration, testing, and publishing review.
skill.summary: Build secure Chrome MV3 extensions with AI integration, privacy review, testing guidance, and publishing checks.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a high score, but most eval, weak crypto, external command, and obfuscation matches are false positives from Markdown checklists, examples, template strings, or PNG icon data. The confirmed risk is medium because the sample extension requests broad host access, reads active page metadata, stores a user API key in browser session storage, and can send page metadata to OpenAI.

medium_findings.title: Broad Page Access and Content Collection
medium_findings.description: The manifest grants http and https host access and loads a content script on matching pages. The content script collects URL, title, description, headings, image metadata, link metadata, and word count, so users need a clear consent and privacy notice.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly grants broad host access and collects page metadata. The behavior is legitimate for an SEO analyzer, but it has meaningful privacy impact.

medium_findings.title: External AI Request With Page Metadata
medium_findings.description: The extension can send page URL, title, description, headings, and word count to the OpenAI API using the stored user API key. This is expected AI functionality, but it exposes page context to a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch call and request body are explicit, and the body includes page-derived metadata. No malicious exfiltration is evident because the destination matches the documented OpenAI feature.

medium_findings.title: Browser Storage of API Key
medium_findings.description: The popup sends an API key to the background worker, which stores and retrieves it from chrome.storage.session. Session storage is safer than persistent local storage, but the extension still handles a sensitive credential.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential flow is directly visible. The implementation uses session storage and only sends the key to OpenAI, so the risk is moderate rather than high.

low_findings.title: Markdown Security Examples Misclassified as Code Execution
low_findings.description: Multiple eval and weak crypto alerts are references inside guidance that says not to use eval, remote code, or unsafe storage. These are documentation examples, not executable behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is security guidance and red-flag criteria. I did not find evidence that it executes code or instructs the agent to bypass security analysis.

low_findings.title: innerHTML Alert Is a Safe Clear Operation or Static Example
low_findings.description: The popup uses innerHTML only to clear a result container before creating elements with textContent. The blueprint innerHTML sample injects static UI markup and does not include untrusted HTML.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The live code uses textContent for dynamic output, which avoids HTML injection. The documentation sample should still prefer DOM APIs, but no direct XSS path is shown.

low_findings.title: Build-Time File Operations Are Scoped to Project Assets
low_findings.description: The filesystem alerts come from scripts that copy static files and decode bundled icon assets. Paths are derived from the script directory and fixed project filenames, so no user-controlled path traversal was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts perform expected build tasks with fixed source and target paths. I did not find command execution or untrusted path input in these files.

low_findings.title: Base64 Icon Data Misclassified as Obfuscation
low_findings.description: The high-entropy strings are PNG icon definitions used by the icon generation script. This is encoded asset data, not an encoded executable payload.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The decoder writes PNG icon files and does not evaluate the decoded bytes. The asset names and usage are consistent with normal Chrome extension icons.

dangerous_patterns.title: All-HTTP-and-HTTPS Host Permissions
dangerous_patterns.description: The extension requests broad host permissions and injects a content script on http and https pages. This pattern should be narrowed when the extension has a known domain scope.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wildcard host patterns are explicit in the manifest. They are common for universal analyzers, but they increase privacy and review risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party AI API Submission
dangerous_patterns.description: The utility function posts a page-derived prompt to the OpenAI chat completions endpoint. This requires consent, disclosure, and careful data minimization.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The request body and Authorization header are visible. The destination is expected for the feature, but it is still sensitive third-party data transfer.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Credential Handling in Extension Runtime
dangerous_patterns.description: The extension accepts and stores a user API key in chrome.storage.session, then reads it for API calls. This is not malicious, but it should be documented and reviewed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential path is direct and easy to trace. Session storage reduces persistence risk but does not remove the need for disclosure.

content.value_statement: Chrome extension projects often fail on permissions, privacy, or Manifest V3 architecture. This skill guides secure design, TypeScript implementation, AI integration, testing, and publishing review.
actual_capabilities: Designs Manifest V3 extension architecture with background, content, popup, offscreen, and shared modules.
Maps Chrome permissions and host access to requested extension features.
Provides TypeScript implementation patterns for messaging, storage, UI, and service worker flows.
Guides optional OpenAI or local AI integration with consent and data minimization checks.
Adds security, privacy, performance, testing, and publishing review checklists.
limitations: It does not guarantee Chrome Web Store approval or legal compliance.
It requires current policy verification before publishing an extension.
It may produce broad-permission examples that need project-specific narrowing.
It does not replace a professional security review for sensitive user data.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Extension
use_cases.description: Turn a feature idea into an MV3 architecture, permission plan, component map, and implementation checklist.

use_cases.title: Migrate MV2 to MV3
use_cases.description: Replace legacy background pages with service workers, update permissions, and review lifecycle risks.

use_cases.title: Review AI Extension Privacy
use_cases.description: Assess page data collection, API key handling, OpenAI calls, consent flows, and store disclosure needs.

prompt_templates.title: Start an Extension Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Chrome Manifest V3 extension for this goal: [goal]. Include components, permissions, data flow, and first implementation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create the MV3 File Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TypeScript MV3 file structure for [extension idea]. Include background, content, popup, shared contracts, and build notes.

prompt_templates.title: Review Permissions and Privacy
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this extension design for least privilege, user consent, API key handling, network calls, and Chrome Web Store disclosure risks.

prompt_templates.title: Debug an Advanced MV3 Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Debug this MV3 issue: [symptoms]. Analyze service worker lifecycle, messaging, storage, offscreen documents, and testing strategy.

output_examples.input: Design an AI SEO analyzer extension for Chrome.
output: Architecture summary with popup, content script, service worker, shared contracts, and optional offscreen parser.
Permission rationale for activeTab, storage, tabs, scripting, host access, and API connectivity.
Privacy checklist covering consent, page metadata transfer, and API key storage.
Risk review for permissions, content script scope, third-party requests, credential handling, and CSP.
Action list for narrowing host permissions, adding consent copy, and testing service worker behavior.
Publishing notes that separate likely compliant items from items that need policy verification.

best_practices: Start with least-privilege permissions and expand only when a feature requires it.
Document every data flow before adding AI calls or persistent storage.
Verify Chrome Web Store policies shortly before release.
anti_patterns: Using all URLs, tabs, or scripting permissions without a clear user-facing reason.
Sending page content to an AI service without consent and disclosure.
Claiming guaranteed store approval without current policy review.

faq.question: Does this skill build complete Chrome extensions?
faq.answer: It guides architecture, code patterns, testing, and review. The final extension still needs project-specific implementation and verification.

faq.question: Can it help with Manifest V2 migration?
faq.answer: Yes. It can plan MV3 service workers, permissions, messaging changes, and lifecycle testing.

faq.question: Does it support AI features?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers OpenAI and local AI patterns, with consent, API key handling, and data minimization checks.

faq.question: Is it safe for privacy-sensitive extensions?
faq.answer: Use it with strict review. It can identify privacy risks, but sensitive products need independent security and legal review.

faq.question: Can it guarantee Chrome Web Store approval?
faq.answer: No. It can prepare disclosure and review checklists, but policy decisions can change and must be verified.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is prepared for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
